text: Last class you delivered the pitch and answered every hard question.
Today you write the follow-up email, where tone is the whole message.

Could you send me the contract?
I was wondering if you could confirm the date.
I would appreciate it if you could reply by Friday.
Would you mind sending the attachment?
I drafted the email to the partner. The first line is: Send me the contract and confirm the date.
That is too blunt for the recipient. He is senior, so the register has to go up. Soften the request.
Understood. I was wondering if you could send the signed contract, and I would appreciate it if you could confirm the date.
Much better. Now open with a proper salutation, and keep the rest concise. Where does the proposal go?
I open with Dear Mr. Costa, and I say the proposal is in the attachment. Then I close with a courteous sign-off.
That is a professional email. Same message, but now the tone matches the recipient. Send it.
Dear Mr. Costa, I hope this email finds you well.
I was wondering if you could send the signed contract.
I would appreciate it if you could confirm the date.
Please find the proposal in the attachment below.
Best regards, Eduardo Chiba, Head of B2B Unit.

Hi Eduardo, it is Sarah. I am leaving you a quick voice note instead of typing, but please turn this into a proper email to the new partner. Keep it formal and concise. Start with a courteous greeting, something like Dear Mr. Costa. Then say that we were wondering if his team could send the signed contract by Friday. Add that we would appreciate it if he could confirm the kickoff date as well. Mention that the proposal is in the attachment, and ask him to let us know if anything is unclear. Close with a polite sign-off, Best regards, and your name and title. Please keep the recipient in mind. He is senior, so no slang and no one-line replies. Thanks.

Let me think about tone before I hit send. The same message can sound very different depending on the register. To a close colleague I might write, can you send me the file. That is fine internally, short and direct. But to a senior partner I have never met, that line is too blunt. I should soften it. Could you send me the file, or even, I was wondering if you could send me the file. The meaning is the same, but the second one is courteous. A good email is concise, but never cold. I open with the right salutation, I make my request politely, I point to the attachment, and I close with a clear sign-off. Tone is not decoration. To the recipient, tone is the message.

First, you open with a courteous salutation that fits the recipient. Then you state your purpose in one concise line. After that, you make your request politely, softening it with could you or I was wondering if. Next, you point the reader to the attachment and any next step. Finally, you close with a polite sign-off and your name.

For a senior partner, use a formal register.
Open with a courteous salutation, such as Dear Mr. Costa.
Keep the message concise so the reader gets the point fast.
Close with a polite sign-off like Best regards.
Could you send the signed contract by Friday?
I would appreciate it if you could reply by Friday.
The proposal is in the attachment. Best regards.

I was wondering if you could send the signed contract.
I would appreciate it if you could confirm the date.
Please find the proposal in the attachment below.
Best regards, Eduardo Chiba, Head of B2B Unit.
